items.q: Can a definite integral give a negative value, even when integrating a function from left to right?
items.correct: Yes — if the function is below the x-axis over the interval, the integral is negative.
items.misconception: Definite integrals are always positive because area cannot be negative.
items.random_wrong: Definite integrals are negative only when the limits are reversed.
items.why: Students equate integrals with geometric area and forget the integral measures signed area.

items.correct: 9 — squaring a negative gives a positive.
items.misconception: (-3)^2 equals -9 because the minus is preserved.
items.random_wrong: (-3)^2 equals -6 because the negative makes you subtract.
items.why: Students confuse (-3)^2 with -3^2; without brackets the second is indeed -9, but with brackets the negative is squared too.

items.misconception: 14, because you read and calculate left to right (3 + 4 = 7, then 7 x 2).
items.random_wrong: 9, because you add all the numbers and then subtract the operator count.
items.why: Students compute left-to-right by default, ignoring the precedence rules in BIDMAS/BODMAS.
The answer examiners want is: x = 4. “x = 8” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's 3. “e³” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
